Notes: Brewed by Nagayama Takahiro who also studied wine making. He owns a winery and a brewery.

During World War II, the worsening war situation led the government to order the brewery to suspend operations and use its facilities to house evacuated schoolchildren. Even after the war ended, the brewery was not immediately allowed to resume production. After persistent effort by the 2nd owner, permission to resume brewing was finally granted. The brewery’s name, “Heiwa” (meaning “peace”), reflects this desire to make sake in a peaceful time.
Named after a real samurai lord who's nick name was drunken whale because he was big and always drunk.

Brewed by the very first non Japanese master brewer, Philip Harper. It is unpasteurized, uncharcoal filtered, and undiluted. It is also made with the Yamahai method (tradtional method), which causes the yeast to be more tolerant to alcohol. Thus this sake is high ABV (20%).

The Daimon Sake Brewery logo has a large gate as its motif. It represents; The gate to the world of sake. The gate of hospitality that is always open. The gate that connects people and nature, and people and people. The gate that creates and protects a path that will last for generations and will continue into the future. The gate that brings good fortune and peace.
Aged in sub-freezing temperature. This prevents the development of oxidative notes often found in aged sake. The fruitiness is retained while the acidity is mellowed out.
Aged for 12 years. Funky. Like a madeira sherry.
Driest sake in the Minnesota market. Poured in a handful of MICHELIN-starred restaurants in Europe
